original_abstract:
Immigration policies are typically tailored toward the young and middle-aged, and often for sound economic reasons. The primary exceptions to this rule have historically been parental and grandparental sponsorship programs, which allow established immigrants to reunite with the older generations left behind in their countries of origin. For over 150 years, the immigration of elderly family members was an unchallenged component of the law. Viewed as the "least threatening" demographic, older immigrants rarely commit crimes, are unlikely to act as conduits for further "chain migration," and pose little threat to the cultural status quo of the host state.

Despite this, the last several decades have witnessed an unprecedented and largely unremarked assault on these programs, often justified by questionable economic logic. Across various jurisdictions, quotas have been slashed, sponsorship income thresholds have been raised, and health requirements have become increasingly punitive. Some states have even begun to police the specific structure of the applicant’s family as a condition for entry. 

This article analyzes the tightening of these rules since the 1970s across three distinct categories of immigrant-receiving nations: traditional settler states, modern settler states, and liberal states that actively seek to discourage migration. The analysis reveals that the legal, political, and economic justifications for these restrictions are remarkably thin. Ultimately, the growing barriers to elderly immigration are both unjust and irrational, representing a shift in policy that should be fundamentally reconsidered and reversed.
humanized_abstract:
For most of recorded history, the immigration system has been focused more towards the working age populations of young and middle aged individuals, and there have been good reasons for that — the economy is a major factor in this regard. Parent and grandparent sponsorship is one of the only exceptions to this, and it is there that older family members are allowed to join their more assimilated and successful relatives in the country that they left years ago. Immigration of the elderly has been allowed, largely without complaint, for over 150 years. Old people generally do not commit crimes, nor act as a major conduit for other migrants in what is sometimes pejoratively called “chain migration” — although the concept of chain migration is somewhat mislabelled as it is not truly a multi directional process — and therefore they do not present the same kind of challenges to the assimilation of younger, more assimilated populations.

When in recent years it was argued that public assistance programs should be abolished in order to cut the state budget, the fact that these are not simple handouts, but a complex system, was ignored in favor of ideology. The reality now is that over the last few decades, aid programs to disadvantaged and vulnerable individuals, including public assistance for family aid, housing subsidies, free medical care, and public educational institutions have all been subject to unprecedented attack in a virtually unnoticed wave. Eligibility limits have been sharply reduced; expectations for raising funds through sponsorship, donations and charitable events have become increasingly unreasonable; and restrictions on use of medical services have grown much tighter. Some states have even made the family composition of the applicants who are eligible for assistance another focus of enforcement.

OPEN DEBATE | Our Debates Are Not Open A recent paper published by the Cato Institute looks at the rules governing admission of older immigrants and finds them to be stricter than they have ever been before, at least since the 1970s. It breaks the rules down into three categories of receiving countries: traditional settler states (e.g., Canada and the US), modern settler states that resemble the US (such as Australia and New Zealand), and liberal (social democratic) states that disagree with the idea of mass immigration (notably Sweden and the UK). The justifications for these laws seem to be minimal to none. Overall the new immigration rules for the elderly are unfair and also unconscionable. As much as one disagrees with current immigration policy the new rules need to be not just reconsidered but the present policy abolished.
